items.misconception: 3/7, by adding the numerators and the denominators separately.
items.why: Students apply the (correct) fraction multiplication rule to addition, treating the numerator and denominator as independent.

items.q: What is the reciprocal of the mixed number 2 1/3?
items.correct: 3/7 — first convert to the improper fraction 7/3, then flip it.
items.misconception: 1/2 1/3, by flipping each part of the mixed number separately.
items.why: Students don't convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before taking the reciprocal, and confuse 'reciprocal' with 'negative'.

items.misconception: 15, because 15% means 15 units out of any amount.
items.why: Students treat 'X percent of' as if X is the answer directly, not as a proportion to apply to the amount.
Correct answer: 3n + 1. “3n” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Using nth = 2n + 5, what is the 3rd term?
items.why: Correct answer: 11. “13” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 18. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
